title: How to Set Up a Solo 401(k) for Your LLC

author.name: Ben Carter
author.bio: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Establish your Solo 401(k) with its own dedicated EIN before December 31st to secure all contribution options for the tax year.
Maximize contributions by funding the plan as both 'employee' (up to the personal IRS limit) and 'employer' (up to 25% of your W-2 salary for an S-Corp).
Anticipate that hiring a W-2 employee working over 1,000 hours per year will disqualify your plan, requiring you to convert or terminate it beforehand.
Leverage advanced features unavailable in IRAs, such as taking a tax-free loan of up to $50,000 or executing a 'Mega Backdoor Roth' contribution for massive tax-free growth.
summary: For solo entrepreneurs, the main problem is treating the Solo 401(k) as a simple savings account, thereby failing to leverage its full strategic power. The core advice is to manage your plan like a "Business-of-One" by adopting the distinct roles of CEO for vision, CFO for financial tactics, and COO for operational compliance. Following this C-suite approach transforms the account from a passive fund into a dynamic wealth-building engine, enabling maximum contributions, advanced tax strategies, and greater control over your financial future.
ai_overview_answer: A Solo 401(k) is a retirement plan designed for self-employed individuals and single-person LLCs, allowing the owner to make tax-advantaged contributions as both an "employee" and an "employer." Establishing a plan involves choosing a provider, formally adopting the plan documents before the December 31st deadline, and obtaining a dedicated Employer Identification Number (EIN) for the plan's trust. With an EIN, you can open the plan's financial accounts and fund them with annual contributions or by rolling over assets from previous retirement accounts. The plan's key feature is its dual contribution structure, which includes an employee salary deferral and a separate employer profit-sharing contribution, up to a combined annual IRS limit. Critical compliance rules include avoiding prohibited transactions like self-dealing and understanding that hiring a non-spouse employee who works over 1,000 hours per year can disqualify the plan. An annual informational return, Form 5500-EZ, must be filed with the IRS for any year the plan's total assets exceed $250,000. Many Solo 401(k) plans also offer advanced features unavailable in SEP IRAs, such as a loan provision and a Roth contribution option for tax-free growth.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

itemListElement.name: How to Set Up a Solo 401(k) for Your LLC

@graph.description: A guide for single-person LLC owners on establishing, managing, and optimizing a Solo 401(k) for maximum wealth building and tax efficiency.

step.name: The CFO's Playbook: Establishing Your Financial Control Center

itemListElement.name: Step 1: Choose Your Provider Wisely
itemListElement.text: This is your most critical decision, as your provider dictates your investment universe and degree of control. You have two primary paths: Large, Traditional Brokerages (Fidelity, Schwab) for public assets, or Specialized 'Self-Directed' Providers (IRA Financial) for alternative investments like real estate. Your choice should be a direct reflection of your long-term investment thesis.

itemListElement.name: Step 2: Formally Establish the Plan and Master the Deadlines
itemListElement.text: Sign the plan adoption agreement from your provider to formally create the retirement trust. The critical deadline to make employee contributions for a tax year is December 31st. Missing this date means forfeiting a year of significant tax deductions and wealth compounding.

itemListElement.name: Step 3: Obtain a Dedicated EIN for the Plan
itemListElement.text: Your Solo 401(k) is a legal trust and requires its own Employer Identification Number (EIN) from the IRS. Do not use your LLC's EIN or your personal SSN. A dedicated EIN is essential for maintaining legal separation and opening financial accounts in the trust's name.

itemListElement.name: Step 4: Open and Fund the Account(s)
itemListElement.text: With the plan established and EIN in hand, open the financial accounts. Fund the plan by making contributions from your LLC's business account or by initiating a direct rollover of funds from previous employers' 401(k)s or existing IRAs.

step.name: The COO's Mandate: A Bulletproof Guide to Compliance

itemListElement.name: The 'Employee Trap' Pre-Mortem
itemListElement.text: The single greatest threat to your plan's 'Solo' status is hiring. The moment you bring on a non-owner, non-spouse W-2 employee who works more than 1,000 hours in a 12-month period, your plan is generally disqualified. Plan your business growth with this constraint in mind.

itemListElement.name: Avoiding Prohibited Transactions
itemListElement.text: The IRS is unequivocal: you cannot engage in 'self-dealing.' This means you cannot use your retirement plan to transact with 'disqualified persons'—a group including yourself, your spouse, your parents, and your children. Violations can trigger severe penalties.

itemListElement.name: Mastering the Form 5500-EZ Filing
itemListElement.text: This annual informational return is required for any year that your plan's total assets exceed $250,000 on the last day of the plan year. The form is due by July 31st of the following year. The penalty for late filing is severe.

mainEntity.name: What is the deadline to open a Solo 401(k) for an S-Corp LLC?

acceptedAnswer.text: To make *employee* contributions for the current tax year, you must establish the plan by December 31st. However, you can make the *employer* profit-sharing contribution up until your business's tax filing deadline (March 15th, or September 15th with an extension). This gives you valuable time post-year-end to assess profitability and optimize your tax-deductible employer contribution.

mainEntity.name: Solo 401(k) vs. SEP IRA for an LLC: which is better?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the founder focused on maximizing contributions and flexibility, the Solo 401(k) is almost always the superior tool. A SEP IRA only allows employer contributions, while a Solo 401(k)'s dual employee/employer structure often allows for larger total contributions, especially at lower income levels. The Solo 401(k) also offers features a SEP IRA lacks, such as a loan provision and a Roth option.

mainEntity.name: What happens to my Solo 401(k) if I hire an employee?

acceptedAnswer.text: Once you hire a W-2 employee who works more than 1,000 hours in a 12-month period, your business generally no longer qualifies for a Solo 401(k). Before they cross this threshold, you must either terminate the plan and roll the assets into an IRA, or convert it into a standard 401(k) that includes all eligible employees, which introduces more complex administration and testing.

mainEntity.name: Can I contribute the max to a Solo 401(k) and a regular 401(k)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, by understanding the separate limits. The *employee* contribution limit ($23,500 in 2025) is a personal limit shared across all plans. If you contribute $15,000 to a day-job 401(k), you can only contribute another $8,500 as an 'employee' to your Solo 401(k). However, the *employer* contribution is separate for each business. Your LLC can still make a full employer profit-sharing contribution to your Solo 401(k) based on its compensation, regardless of your other employment.

mainEntity.name: Can a single-member LLC have a Solo 401(k)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Absolutely. A single-member LLC is an ideal candidate. Eligibility requires self-employment activity and the absence of full-time, non-spouse employees. Your LLC's tax status—whether as a sole proprietorship or an S-Corp—simply changes how your maximum contribution is calculated, not your fundamental eligibility.

body.content: The Business-of-One: A CEO, CFO, and COO's Guide to the Solo 401(k)

For the elite professional running a single-person LLC, the Solo 401(k) is not merely a retirement account; it is the financial control center of your enterprise. Viewing it as a simple savings bucket is a strategic error. To unlock its full potential, you must adopt the mindset of a complete C-Suite for your "Business-of-One."

You must be the **CEO**, setting the long-term vision for your financial independence. You must be the **CFO**, mastering the tactical details of capital allocation, tax efficiency, and investment strategy. And you must be the **COO**, ensuring flawless operational execution and mitigating compliance risk.
This playbook is designed to guide you through each of these roles, transforming your Solo 401(k) from a passive account into the most powerful wealth-building engine in your arsenal.
The CFO's Playbook: Establishing Your Financial Control Center

Your first mandate as CFO is to construct the financial infrastructure. Establishing your Solo 401(k) isn't administrative overhead; it's a foundational act of capital allocation. The process is straightforward when broken down into a deliberate, logical sequence.
Step 1: Choose Your Provider Wisely
This is your most critical decision, as your provider dictates your investment universe and degree of control. You have two primary paths:

items: **Large, Traditional Brokerages:** Firms like Fidelity or Charles Schwab offer streamlined, low-cost setups ideal for strategies centered on public assets like stocks, bonds, and ETFs. The trade-off is a general prohibition on alternative investments.

**Specialized "Self-Directed" Providers:** Firms like IRA Financial are built for sophisticated investors who intend to use their plan to invest in real estate, private equity, or other non-traditional assets. They offer "checkbook control" and compliance support for complex transactions, typically at a higher fee.

body.content: Your choice should be a direct reflection of your long-term investment thesis.
Step 2: Formally Establish the Plan and Master the Deadlines

This is the official act of creating your retirement trust. Your provider will supply a plan adoption agreement and other legal documents; signing them formally "establishes" the plan. Deadlines are a primary source of compliance risk.

items: **The Critical Deadline:** To make *employee* contributions for a tax year, the plan must be established by December 31st. While recent legislation offers some flexibility for employer-only contributions, adhering to the year-end deadline is the most conservative approach, ensuring all contribution options remain available. Missing this date means forfeiting a year of significant tax deductions and wealth compounding.

body.content: Step 3: Obtain a Dedicated EIN for the Plan

This step separates the professional from the amateur. Your Solo 401(k) is a legal trust, distinct from you and your LLC, and requires its own Employer Identification Number (EIN) from the IRS. **Do not use your LLC's EIN or your personal Social Security Number.** A dedicated EIN is essential for maintaining legal separation, opening financial accounts in the trust's name, and executing investments correctly. Your provider can typically assist with this free and instantaneous online process.
Step 4: Open and Fund the Account(s)

With the plan established and EIN in hand, you can open the actual financial accounts. You will typically open separate accounts for each contribution type (e.g., pre-tax, Roth). Funding occurs in two ways:

items: **Contributions:** Transfer money from your LLC's business account to the new plan account to make your annual employee and employer contributions.

**Rollovers:** Consolidate your financial life by initiating a direct rollover of funds from previous employers' 401(k)s or existing IRAs. This brings assets under a single roof that you control, simplifying management and unlocking more advanced strategies.

body.content: The COO's Mandate: A Bulletproof Guide to Compliance

Once the plan is funded, your mindset must pivot from accumulation to protection. As the COO of your enterprise, your primary mandate is mitigating risk. The power of the Solo 401(k) is balanced by an equal measure of responsibility to adhere to strict IRS regulations. This is the operational checklist to ensure your plan remains a strategic asset, not a liability.

items: **The "Employee Trap" Pre-Mortem:** The single greatest threat to your plan's "Solo" status is hiring. The moment you bring on a non-owner, non-spouse W-2 employee who works more than 1,000 hours in a 12-month period, your plan is generally disqualified. Plan your business growth with this constraint in mind. If you anticipate needing help, structure those engagements meticulously as independent contractors with clear contracts and scopes of work. If hiring a full-time employee becomes necessary, you must act *before* they cross the 1,000-hour threshold to either convert your plan to a standard 401(k) or terminate it.

**Avoiding Prohibited Transactions:** The IRS is unequivocal: you cannot engage in "self-dealing." This means you cannot use your retirement plan to transact with "disqualified persons"—a group including yourself, your spouse, your parents, and your children. Selling a property you personally own to your 401(k) or having the plan lend you money without a formal loan agreement can trigger severe penalties, starting with a 15% excise tax and escalating to a 100% penalty if uncorrected.

**Mastering the Form 5500-EZ Filing:** This annual informational return is a simple compliance checkpoint. You are only required to file it with the IRS for any year that your plan's total assets exceed $250,000 on the last day of the plan year. Set a calendar reminder for early January to check your year-end balance. If you are over the threshold, the form is due by July 31st of the following year. The penalty for late filing is severe, starting at $250 per day.

body.content: The CEO's Mandate: Engineering Wealth and Advanced Strategy

With the operational guardrails in place, you can step into the CEO role. Your focus now shifts from defense to offense—from saving to strategic wealth creation. A Solo 401(k) is an active engine for reinvesting profits with maximum capital efficiency.
Master the Dual Contribution Structure
The power of a Solo 401(k) lies in your ability to contribute as two separate entities: the "employee" and the "employer."

items: **As the Employee:** You can contribute up to 100% of your compensation, capped at the annual IRS limit ($23,500 in 2025). If you are age 50 or over, you can add a "catch-up" contribution.
**As the Employer:** Your LLC can make a profit-sharing contribution of up to 25% of your compensation (for an S-Corp) or approximately 20% of net self-employment income (for a sole proprietorship).

body.content: The combined total cannot exceed the overall IRS limit ($70,000 in 2025, plus any catch-up amounts).

rows: A personal, dollar-for-dollar contribution from your salary.
A percentage-based contribution from the business.
The ceiling for both contribution types combined.

body.content: For an LLC taxed as an S-Corporation, you must pay yourself a **reasonable W-2 salary**. The employer contribution is calculated based *only* on this salary, not on additional profit distributions. The strategic imperative is to set a salary that is both compliant and high enough to allow for a substantial employer contribution.
Unlock Advanced Tactical Tools
Beyond contributions, your plan offers tools for granular control and profound tax efficiency.

items: **The "Mega Backdoor Roth" Maneuver:** If your plan documents allow, you can make voluntary *after-tax* contributions above and beyond your standard limits, right up to the overall IRS ceiling. You then immediately execute an in-plan conversion of these funds into the Roth component of your plan. The result is a massive infusion into your tax-free growth engine, shielding tens of thousands in future gains from taxes.

**Strategic Roth vs. Traditional Contributions:** Your choice between Roth (after-tax) and Traditional (pre-tax) contributions should be a deliberate, annual decision based on your current versus projected future income.
**Use Roth** if you expect to be in a *higher* tax bracket in retirement. Pay taxes now to secure tax-free growth and withdrawals later.
**Use Traditional** if you need to maximize *current* tax deductions or anticipate a lower tax bracket in retirement. Defer the tax liability to maximize available capital today.

**The Plan Loan Provision:** Unlike an IRA, a Solo 401(k) allows you to borrow up to 50% of your vested balance, capped at $50,000. This provides short-term, tax-free liquidity for your business or personal needs. The interest you pay is paid directly back into your own retirement account, preserving the compounding growth of your core assets.

**Alternative Investments for True Diversification:** A self-directed plan empowers you to break away from Wall Street and invest directly in assets like real estate, private equity, precious metals, and cryptocurrency. This allows you to build a portfolio not directly correlated with public market volatility, a hallmark of sophisticated institutional investing.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
As you move from theory to practice, specific questions will arise. Here are clear answers to the most common queries.

items:
**What is the deadline to open a Solo 401(k) for an S-Corp LLC?**
To make *employee* contributions for the current tax year, you must establish the plan by December 31st. However, you can make the *employer* profit-sharing contribution up until your business's tax filing deadline (March 15th, or September 15th with an extension). This gives you valuable time post-year-end to assess profitability and optimize your tax-deductible employer contribution.

**Solo 401(k) vs. SEP IRA for an LLC: which is better?**
For the founder focused on maximizing contributions and flexibility, the Solo 401(k) is almost always the superior tool. A SEP IRA only allows employer contributions, while a Solo 401(k)'s dual employee/employer structure often allows for larger total contributions, especially at lower income levels. The Solo 401(k) also offers features a SEP IRA lacks, such as a loan provision and a Roth option.

**What happens to my Solo 401(k) if I hire an employee?**
Once you hire a W-2 employee who works more than 1,000 hours in a 12-month period, your business generally no longer qualifies for a Solo 401(k). Before they cross this threshold, you must either terminate the plan and roll the assets into an IRA, or convert it into a standard 401(k) that includes all eligible employees, which introduces more complex administration and testing.

**Can I contribute the max to a Solo 401(k) and a regular 401(k)?**
Yes, by understanding the separate limits. The *employee* contribution limit ($23,500 in 2025) is a personal limit shared across all plans. If you contribute $15,000 to a day-job 401(k), you can only contribute another $8,500 as an "employee" to your Solo 401(k). However, the *employer* contribution is separate for each business. Your LLC can still make a full employer profit-sharing contribution to your Solo 401(k) based on its compensation, regardless of your other employment.

**Can a single-member LLC have a Solo 401(k)?**
Absolutely. A single-member LLC is an ideal candidate. Eligibility requires self-employment activity and the absence of full-time, non-spouse employees. Your LLC's tax status—whether as a sole proprietorship or an S-Corp—simply changes how your maximum contribution is calculated, not your fundamental eligibility.

body.content: Your Financial Future is Your Ultimate Enterprise

By embracing the roles of CEO, CFO, and COO for your Business-of-One, you transform your Solo 401(k) from a simple account into your most powerful strategic asset. The CEO sets the vision of financial independence. The CFO executes with tactical precision, optimizing every tax and investment decision. The COO protects the entire enterprise with disciplined, risk-averse operations.

This integrated approach is how you move from simply running a business to building a legacy. Your financial future is your ultimate responsibility, and this plan is your primary instrument of control. Wield it accordingly.
